grader_components.part: German identity hardened through contrast with Slavic neighbours (the wider pattern / thesis)
the specific 'episode' fits this wider pattern (generalization from one case)
institutional weakness, more than settled nationhood, made such rhetoric useful (causal/interpretive reading)

grader_missing_subclaim: Every component — the pattern of German identity hardening through contrast with Slavic neighbours, the episode's fit to that pattern, and the causal claim that institutional weakness rather than settled nationhood made the rhetoric useful — is unsupported.
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Cite a source that establishes both the pattern of German identity defined against Slavic neighbours and the argument that institutional weakness (rather than settled nationhood) drove such rhetoric, or narrow the claim to what a cited source actually states.
grader_why: No source text was provided, so none of the claim's components — including its interpretive thesis and its causal 'weakness more than nationhood' reading — can be proven from the cited source. Because the proof does not exist in any provided text, this is the author's problem to fix (add a citation or rewrite), not a matter of the tool showing the wrong sentences. The claim is heavily interpretive, and per rule A those generalizations are components held to the same standard as facts.
fable_reasoning: reason=source_file_missing: scales2012.pdf. German-identity-through-contrast claim not checkable without the source. Rubric: source missing -> not_rulable.

grader_components.part: Sachsenspiegel's electoral-prince list omitted the Bohemian king from those voting first
the stated ground that he was "not German"
philological analysis dates that clause to Rudolf of Habsburg's disputed election on 1 October 1273
Ottokar II's proxy vote was set aside at that election

grader_missing_subclaim: the entire claim — every component (the Sachsenspiegel omission, the "not German" ground, the philological dating to the 1 October 1273 election of Rudolf of Habsburg, and the setting-aside of Ottokar II's proxy vote)
grader_why: No source text was provided and no sentences were shown, so nothing in the claim can be located, quoted, or verified. With no provided text, no component is provable, so this cannot be a tool-display problem (which requires the proof to exist in the source); it defaults to an author-side fix — a citation must be supplied (or the claim rewritten to what a real source supports) before support can be assessed.
fable_reasoning: reason=source_file_missing: wolf2020.pdf. The Sachsenspiegel/Rudolf-1273 dating claim cannot be checked with no source present. Rubric: source missing -> not_rulable. Pipeline's 'unsupported' is a can't-verify technicality, not a substantive refutation.

grader_components.part: Princes whose autonomy rested on a weak crown had reason to resist a stronger successor (interpretive thesis about elector motive)
After Rudolf died in 1291 the electors bypassed Albert for Adolf of Nassau
explicitly to forestall a hereditary Habsburg dynasty

grader_missing_subclaim: Princes whose autonomy rested on a weak crown had reason to resist a stronger successor
grader_rewrite_suggestion: After Rudolf died in 1291 the electors bypassed Albert for Adolf of Nassau, hoping to nip the emergence of a hereditary Habsburg dynasty in the bud.
grader_proofs_verified: After the death of King Rudolf I in 1291 it was evident that the efforts made by the latter to establish the title as a hereditary office in the Habsburg dynasty had come to nought.
The electors chose not Albrecht but Adolf of Nassau as the new Roman-German king, hoping to nip the emergence of a royal Habsburg dynasty in the bud.
grader_why: The shown sentences fully prove the two factual components — the 1291 succession bypass of Albrecht for Adolf and the electors' aim of forestalling a hereditary Habsburg dynasty. But the leading clause is an interpretive generalization (princes with autonomy resting on a weak crown had a structural motive to resist a stronger successor) that the source never states or establishes; it discusses only the electors' specific fear of a Habsburg dynasty, not a general theory of princely resistance. Per Rule A this interpretive component is a claim held to the same standard, so the author must add a citation or rewrite down to what the source supports.
fable_reasoning: The specific example (1291 Rudolf dies, electors bypass Albert for Adolf to forestall a hereditary Habsburg dynasty) is proven verbatim. The lead clause — the general causal principle that 'princes whose autonomy rested on a weak crown had reason to resist a stronger successor' — is the author's generalization extrapolated from the example, not stated by the source (arbiter concurs). Author synthesis resting on a correctly-cited fact -> own_interpretation.

grader_components.part: By 1273 Ottokar ruled Austria, Styria, Carinthia and Carniola besides Bohemia and Moravia
Ottokar was the wealthiest prince in the empire

grader_missing_subclaim: that Ottokar was the wealthiest prince in the empire
grader_rewrite_suggestion: By 1273 Ottokar ruled Austria, Styria, Carinthia and Carniola besides Bohemia and Moravia and was by far the most important of the electors, so they chose the little-known Rudolf of Habsburg to check that dominance.
grader_proofs_verified: He also commanded Carinthia and Carniola.
His election came as a surprise to him, as he was not among the most powerful princes of the Empire.
King Ottokar II Přemysl of Bohemia (c. 1232–1278) was by far the most important of the electors and regarded himself as the natural head of the Empire.
grader_why: The source proves the territorial list, but only Bohemia, Moravia, Austria and Styria appear in the shown sentence — Carinthia and Carniola exist in the source ("He also commanded Carinthia and Carniola") yet are not displayed, and "obscure" is provable ("not among the most powerful princes") but likewise unshown. Critically, the source calls Ottokar "by far the most important of the electors," never the "wealthiest prince in the empire"; it in fact says Rudolf was the wealthy one, so the wealth characterization is the author's addition not provable from this source. Because at least one component cannot be sourced here, the author must fix it, even though the other components' proof exists in the text.
fable_reasoning: Components: Ottokar's territories + electors chose Rudolf to check his dominance (proven; source: 'by far the most important of the electors', rise 'too rapid a pace'), and the superlative 'wealthiest prince in the empire' (uncovered). Source says most important/powerful, never 'wealthiest' — a specific ranking not stated. Core proven + one component unsourced -> partial.

grader_why: No source text was provided at all, so nothing in the claim can be verified against the cited source, and no proof sentences can be quoted. Because the components are not provable from the provided text (rather than merely mis-displayed), this is an author-side problem. The remedy is to supply a citation whose text establishes these components or to rewrite the claim down to what a real source supports.
fable_reasoning: reason=source_file_missing: antonin2017.pdf, kalhous2023.pdf. St-Wenceslas-cult legitimation claim not checkable; the claim itself is hedged ('remains interpretive'). Rubric: source missing -> not_rulable.
